techniques.evidence_quote: a step in the GitHub Actions workflow inadvertently wrote the GitHub Actions Runner's environment variables, including sensitive AWS access keys, to a JavaScript file. The JavaScript file containing the keys was subsequently deployed and served publicly as part of TinaCloud, allowing attackers to obtain the access keys directly from the front-end code.
AWS access keys with root permissions were compromised and misused
Based off CloudTrail logs, there was no evidence of unauthorized access to customer data.
These keys were exploited to send unauthorized emails (targeting the general French community, not Tina customers specifically) using our Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) infrastructure.

attack_chain.description: During the CI/CD build process, a GitHub Actions workflow step inadvertently wrote the runner's environment variables — including AWS access keys with root permissions — into a JavaScript file. This file was then deployed and publicly served as part of the TinaCloud front-end, exposing the credentials to anyone who inspected the JS bundle.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a step in the GitHub Actions workflow inadvertently wrote the GitHub Actions Runner's environment variables, including sensitive AWS access keys, to a JavaScript file. The JavaScript file containing the keys was subsequently deployed and served publicly as part of TinaCloud, allowing attackers to obtain the access keys directly from the front-end code.

attack_chain.description: The attacker harvested the exposed AWS access keys from the publicly served JavaScript file and used them to authenticate to the TinaCloud AWS environment with root-level permissions, beginning unauthorized activity on 13th December 2024.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: AWS access keys with root permissions were compromised and misused

attack_chain.description: Using the compromised root-level AWS access keys, the attacker abused TinaCloud's Amazon SES infrastructure to send unauthorized phishing emails in French to the general French-speaking community, effectively hijacking the company's email-sending resource.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: These keys were exploited to send unauthorized emails (targeting the general French community, not Tina customers specifically) using our Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) infrastructure.

initial_access_vector: AWS root-level access keys inadvertently embedded in a JavaScript file by a GitHub Actions CI/CD build step, then publicly served as part of the TinaCloud front-end application
persistence_mechanism: None identified; the compromised key was revoked as an automated response and all legacy keys were subsequently rotated
privilege_escalation: No escalation required — the exposed credentials already carried root/administrative AWS permissions
